At two o'clock, we were setting up the room.
I was testing the microphones when the news arrived.
While we were changing the schedule, he made a decision.
What were you doing when the minister arrived?
Bruno, I got your message. I need to reconstruct one part. What were you doing when the news about the minister arrived?
I was testing the microphones. Our team was still setting up, and the Korean delegation was waiting outside.
And the rector -- was he in the room when you got the news?
Yes. He was reading the agenda. He made the decision in one minute: start without her.
That is the detail I needed. So while you were changing the schedule, he was already speaking?
Exactly. The first speaker began at two thirty. The whole thing worked, but the sequence changed.
You know, you used to ask me to write these reports for you.
I used to. Not anymore. Today I am telling the whole thing myself -- and my version is accurate.
What were you doing when it happened?
I was preparing the room when the news arrived.
Let me reconstruct the whole thing in sequence.
I used to bring an interpreter. Now I tell the story myself.

Maria, it is Bruno. You asked me to reconstruct the round table in Seoul, so here is my version. At two o'clock, we were setting up the room. Our team was testing the microphones, and the Korean delegation was waiting outside. Everything was ready. Then, at two fifteen, the news arrived: the minister was one hour late. While we were changing the schedule, the rector made a decision. He started the session without her. So the first speaker began at two thirty, not at three. I know you were talking on the phone at that moment, so you missed the beginning. The whole thing worked, but the sequence changed. If you need more details, call me.

Yesterday, I gave my first debrief in English. I used to bring an interpreter to every meeting. For twenty years, someone else was telling my story, and I was sitting in silence next to my own words. Not this time. While the director was listening, I reconstructed the whole thing myself: what we were doing at two o'clock, what happened with the minister, and why the sequence changed. My English was not perfect. I stopped two times, and I asked for one word. But every detail was mine, and my version was accurate. At the end, the director said one sentence: Thank you, that was very clear. In that room, I was not a beginner. I was a leader, and I was speaking for myself.

At two o'clock, we were setting up the room, and the team was testing the microphones. At two fifteen, the news arrived: the minister was one hour late. While we were changing the schedule, the rector made a decision. The first speaker began at two thirty, not at three. In the end, the whole thing worked. Only the sequence changed.
